Title: Synthetic communities derived from the core endophytic microbiome of hyperaccumulators and their role in cadmium phytoremediation

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: phytoremediation target; removal of toxic Cd from contaminated soil
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: As a member of SynCom-NS, contributes to increased plant growth, Cd uptake, and Cd translocation in S. alfredii.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SaLS1 was selected from the core endophytic microbiome-associated genus Leifsonia isolated from hyperaccumulating S. alfredii. 2. It showed plant growth-promoting traits including IAA production and Cd tolerance. 3. Inclusion in the optimized SynCom-NS was associated with enhanced biomass and Cd accumulation in hydroponic and pot experiments.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: strain isolation; trait assay for IAA production/Cd tolerance; SynCom inclusion/exclusion hydroponic experiment; pot inoculation experiment
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cd exposure in hydroponic solution and contaminated soil; SynCom-NS inoculation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as a component of synthetic microbial inoculants for enhancing Cd phytoremediation by S. alfredii.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: genome resequencing of candidate strain under BioProject PRJNA1085268; microbiome 16S data PRJNA1043636 and PRJNA1085113
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No specific negative effect reported for SaLS1 alone.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Leifsonia was identified as a core genus enriched in hyperaccumulator shoots and positively associated with Cd hyperaccumulation.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: phytoremediation target; removal of toxic Cd from contaminated soil
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: As a member of SynCom-NS, contributes to enhanced plant growth and Cd accumulation.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SaNL1 was one of five representative strains selected to construct reduced SynComs after excluding Alcaligenes. 2. It possessed IAA production and Cd tolerance traits. 3. Its presence in SynCom-NS was part of the consortium with the strongest Cd phytoremediation-promoting effect.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: strain isolation; trait assay; genome resequencing; SynCom inclusion/exclusion hydroponic experiment; pot inoculation experiment
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cd exposure in hydroponic solution and contaminated soil; SynCom-NS inoculation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential SynCom component for microbial agents to improve Cd phytoextraction.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: genome resequencing of candidate strain under BioProject PRJNA1085268; microbiome 16S data PRJNA1043636 and PRJNA1085113
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Novosphingobium was selected for strong growth-promoting traits and Cd tolerance rather than prior designation as a core genus.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: phytoremediation target; removal of toxic Cd from contaminated soil
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: As a SynCom-NS member, contributes to increased Cd uptake/translocation and plant growth.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SaOA1 was chosen as the Ochrobactrum representative based on growth-promoting and Cd-tolerance traits. 2. It was retained in the best-performing SynCom-NS consortium. 3. SynCom-NS improved Cd bioconcentration factor, translocation factor, biomass, and tissue Cd levels in S. alfredii.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: strain isolation; trait assay; genome resequencing; SynCom inclusion/exclusion hydroponic experiment; pot inoculation experiment
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cd exposure in hydroponic solution and contaminated soil; SynCom-NS inoculation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate member for constructing microbial consortia for Cd phytoremediation enhancement.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: genome resequencing of candidate strain under BioProject PRJNA1085268; microbiome 16S data PRJNA1043636 and PRJNA1085113
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Ochrobactrum was included due to strong plant growth-promoting traits and Cd tolerance.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: phytoremediation target; removal of toxic Cd from contaminated soil
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: As a SynCom-NS member, contributes to enhanced biomass and Cd accumulation in S. alfredii.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SaPI1 came from the core genus Pseudomonas associated with Cd hyperaccumulation. 2. It displayed plant growth-promoting features and Cd tolerance. 3. Together with SaLS1, SaNL1, and SaOA1 in SynCom-NS, it supported the strongest promotion of Cd uptake and plant growth.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: strain isolation; trait assay; genome resequencing; SynCom inclusion/exclusion hydroponic experiment; pot inoculation experiment
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cd exposure in hydroponic solution and contaminated soil; SynCom-NS inoculation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Valuable microbial inoculant component for improving phytoremediation efficiency.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: genome resequencing of candidate strain under BioProject PRJNA1085268; microbiome 16S data PRJNA1043636 and PRJNA1085113
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Pseudomonas was identified as a core genus in both roots and shoots and discussed as beneficial for nutrient mobilization and growth promotion.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: phytoremediation target; removal of toxic Cd from contaminated soil
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Included in some SynComs but exclusion of SaSP1 generated SynCom-NS, the most effective consortium for increasing Cd uptake and growth.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SaSP1 was selected from the core genus Sphingomonas and had IAA production and Cd tolerance. 2. Genome resequencing suggested it contained the fewest unique growth-promoting genes and lower metabolic exchange potential than other strains. 3. Excluding SaSP1 improved SynCom performance, indicating it was less beneficial than the remaining four strains in this context.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: strain isolation; trait assay; genome resequencing; SynCom inclusion/exclusion hydroponic experiment
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Caution for SynCom design: this strain may be omitted to increase metabolic complementarity and phytoremediation efficacy.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: genome resequencing of candidate strain under BioProject PRJNA1085268
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Its inclusion may reduce SynCom performance relative to SynCom-NS.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Sphingomonas is a core genus, but the representative strain SaSP1 showed weaker contribution within the designed consortium.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: phytoremediation target; removal of toxic Cd from contaminated soil
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Negatively associated with plant Cd concentration and antagonistic to other target genera; excluded from optimized SynCom design.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Alcaligenes was initially included among candidate SynCom taxa because of growth-promoting traits and Cd tolerance. 2. Network analysis after SynCom13 inoculation showed its abundance was negatively correlated with Cd concentration in plant tissues. 3. Alcaligenes also negatively correlated with other target genera and was therefore excluded from further SynCom construction.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: network analysis of inoculated plants; differential abundance analysis; SynCom optimization by exclusion
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Cd contamination; SynCom13 inoculation in sterile soil
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Should be avoided in microbial consortia intended to maximize Cd phytoextraction in S. alfredii.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduces Cd accumulation potential and may antagonize beneficial strains.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussion suggests Alcaligenes can reduce Cd bioavailability through biomineralization.
